messages.content: to login to or to log into

Both "to login" and "to log into" are correct and have the same meaning. They are used interchangeably to describe the process of accessing a computer system or website through an account by providing a username and password. The choice between "to login" and "to log into" is a matter of personal preference or local conventions.
if I use "to login", is it necessary to add "to" behind?

No, it is not necessary to add "to" after "login." "Login" can be used as a verb on its own without the preposition "to." So you can say "I need to login" or "I need to log in." Both are correct and have the same meaning.
what is ARP scan

An ARP scan is a technique used to discover and map devices within a network using the ARP protocol. ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) is used to translate IP addresses to MAC addresses. By sending ARP requests to all devices on a network, an ARP scan can detect the connected devices and gather information such as their IP and MAC addresses. 

ARP scans can be performed by using command-line tools, network scanners, or security tools. The information gathered from an ARP scan can help to identify potential vulnerabilities, unauthorized devices on the network, or outdated systems that need to be updated. However, it can also be used for malicious purposes such as network reconnaissance or unauthorized access to network devices. Therefore, it's important to use it responsibly and only with proper authorization or permission.
Protocols may differ when the device in use is replaced.

This statement is generally true. Protocols are a set of rules and procedures that govern the transmission of data between devices on a network. These protocols are implemented by software and/or hardware on the devices, and can vary depending on the manufacturer, model, and version of the device. 

When a device is replaced, the new device may have a different set of protocols, or the same protocols implemented in a different way, which can cause compatibility issues. This can result in communication problems or even a failure of the devices to communicate altogether. 

To ensure compatibility, it's important to ensure that the devices being used, and the protocols they implement, are compatible and meet the requirements of the application or network. This may involve researching the specifications of the devices, checking with the manufacturer or vendor, or hiring a professional to configure and test the devices before deployment.
can i improve to this: Protocols in display may differ when the device in use is replaced.

Yes, you can improve the sentence to make it more clear. Here are some suggestions:

- "When a device is replaced, the new device may use different protocols than the previous device, which can lead to compatibility issues."
- "Different devices may use different protocols for displaying information on a network, so when a device is replaced, it's important to ensure that the new device uses compatible protocols."
- "Replacing a device on a network can cause protocol mismatches, where the new device may not be able to communicate with other devices on the network due to differences in implemented protocols." 

These suggestions convey that the protocols may differ or clash when a device is replaced, and emphasizes the importance of ensuring that the new device uses compatible protocols.
the protocols are displayed on the web interface

This statement is not necessarily true. The protocols used within a device's web interface depend on the device and the web interface's functionality. The web interface is simply a graphical user interface (GUI) used to interact with the device's configuration, management, and monitoring features. 

While some web interfaces may display information and settings related to network protocols, others may not. For example, a simple web interface for a printer may only display settings related to paper tray selection and print quality, while a web interface for a router may provide options related to network protocols such as DHCP, DNS, or NAT. 

Therefore, it's not accurate to say that protocols are always displayed on the web interface. Rather, it depends on the device's functionality and the information and settings that the web interface provides.
i mean, the protocols are displayed on the web interface. So is it proper if i change the sentence to: Protocols in display may differ when the device in use is replaced.

Yes, if the web interface of the device displays protocols, then it would be correct to say "protocols in display may differ when the device in use is replaced." However, you may want to consider restructuring the sentence to make it clearer, such as: "If protocols are displayed on the device's web interface, replacing the device may result in differences in the protocols displayed." This makes it clearer that the protocols are being displayed on the web interface, and also emphasizes that these differences are related to the protocols that are displayed.
is it clear: Changes made to the WAN settings here will clear customer-defined configurations in the Network menu.

Yes, the sentence is clear and effectively communicates the message. It suggests that if someone makes changes to the WAN settings in this particular area, it will result in the removal of customer-defined configurations in the Network menu. So the person should proceed with caution while making changes to the WAN settings, as it may affect other configurations made in the Network menu.

is it clear: Normally, if the Gateway is using a common 4G module, the device port for 4G/LTE communication displayed under the protocol will be “3g-4g” which is the WAN port. When using a carrier pre-certified 4G module, the device port for 4G/LTE communication displayed under the protocol will be “eth2” which is the WAN port.

Yes, the sentence is clear and effectively communicates the difference in device port display for 4G/LTE communication when using a common 4G module versus a carrier pre-certified 4G module. It explains that normally the device port for 4G/LTE communication displayed under the protocol will be "3g-4g" which is the WAN port when using a common 4G module. However, when using a carrier pre-certified 4G module, the device port for 4G/LTE communication displayed under the protocol will be "eth2" which is the WAN port. This is very helpful for people who want to install or replace a 4G module and want to know which device port to use for communication.

Normally, if the Gateway is using a common 4G module, the device port for 4G/LTE communication displayed under the protocol will be “3g-4g” which is the WAN port. When using a carrier pre-certified 4G module (exclusive for customers based outside China), the device port for 4G/LTE communication displayed under the protocol will be “eth2” which is the WAN port.

Yes, this revised sentence is still clear and effectively communicates the same message as the previous sentence, while also adding clarity that the carrier pre-certified 4G module is exclusive for customers based outside China. This helps provide additional information to the reader and clarity regarding the specifications of the pre-certified 4G module.
